title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Motivated by rumors of pagan behavior, Richard Nix and Ada Gransbury conspired with Harriet Sipper to remove the parish vicar of Glebeholme, Patrick Ives, from his position and expel him from the church. Despite the accusations, Patrick refused to confess, maintaining that the rumors were unfounded. The village's hostility extended to his wife, Francis, who suffered a physical and mental collapse. While most villagers participated in the persecution, Nancy Duane and Dr. Ambrose Flavard refused to join. Flavard publicly defended the couple, arguing that Francis's absence during the alleged incidents proved her innocence and accusing Harriet Sipper of spreading slander.

Harriet eventually informed Victor Mickle of a secret Nancy Duane held, leading to a gathering at the King's Head Inn. During this meeting, Nancy confessed that while Patrick had not paid her to communicate with her deceased husband, she had been meeting him secretly because she was in love with him. She maintained they had no physical contact, claiming their meetings were merely to discuss their impossible feelings. Harriet Sipper responded by vehemently condemning Patrick as a fraudulent, unfaithful pagan. Despite Nancy's attempt to stop the rumors through her confession, Ada Gransbury and Harriet led the villagers in continued condemnation.

The harassment culminated in tragedy when Francis Ives committed suicide by poisoning, and a guilt-stricken Patrick Ives also took his own life. Following these deaths, Richard Nix broke his engagement and left Glebeholme, Nancy Duane moved to London, and the maid responsible for the initial rumors disappeared. New clergy, Charles and Margaret Ernst, eventually arrived to take over the parish.
